quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Probability distribution"?

options.text: A function that assigns probabilities to every possible value of a random variable, with all probabilities summing to 1
Standard computer output provides coefficients (b₀, b₁), standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, and R² for interpretation
The fraction of successes in a sample: p̂ = X/n, where X is the number of successes
An unobserved variable that influences both the explanatory and response variables, creating a false impression of a direct relationship

quiz_questions.explanation: "Probability distribution" is defined as: A function that assigns probabilities to every possible value of a random variable, with all probabilities summing to 1. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Uniform distribution"?

options.text: A variable not included in the study that may influence the relationship between the variables being studied
A distribution where every outcome in the sample space has equal probability
μ = np; the expected number of successes in n trials
SD(X ± Y) = √(Var(X) + Var(Y)) for independent random variables; note that SDs do NOT simply add

quiz_questions.explanation: "Uniform distribution" is defined as: A distribution where every outcome in the sample space has equal probability. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Expected value E(X)"?

options.text: A data point falling more than 1.5 × IQR below Q1 or above Q3, potentially indicating unusual observations or data errors
Random sample or random assignment, n < 10% of population, np₀ ≥ 10 and n(1-p₀) ≥ 10
The long-run average value of a random variable, calculated as the weighted mean of all possible values: E(X) = Σ[x·P(x)]
A scatterplot of residuals versus the explanatory variable; used to check whether a linear model is appropriate for the data

quiz_questions.explanation: "Expected value E(X)" is defined as: The long-run average value of a random variable, calculated as the weighted mean of all possible values: E(X) = Σ[x·P(x)]. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Standard deviation of a random variable"?

options.text: A variable that places individuals into groups or categories (e.g., gender, political party, blood type) and is summarized using counts or proportions
A measure of the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two quantitative variables; ranges from -1 to +1
The square root of variance, measuring the typical deviation of a random variable from its expected value: σ = √Var(X)
A variable associated with both the explanatory variable and the response variable that distorts the apparent relationship between them

quiz_questions.explanation: "Standard deviation of a random variable" is defined as: The square root of variance, measuring the typical deviation of a random variable from its expected value: σ = √Var(X). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Cumulative distribution function (CDF)"?

options.text: F(x) = P(X ≤ x); gives the probability that a random variable takes a value less than or equal to x
Increasing n decreases the margin of error proportionally to 1/√n, producing a narrower (more precise) interval
Compare R² values, residual plots, and prediction accuracy across candidate models to choose the best-fitting transformation
A statistically significant slope may have little practical importance if the effect is very small; always consider R² and the magnitude of b₁

quiz_questions.explanation: "Cumulative distribution function (CDF)" is defined as: F(x) = P(X ≤ x); gives the probability that a random variable takes a value less than or equal to x. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A function that assigns probabilities to every possible value of a random variable, with all probabilities summing to 1"?

options.text: Probability distribution
Establishing causation
Median
Follow-up analysis

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Probability distribution".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A distribution where every outcome in the sample space has equal probability"?

options.text: Replication
Uniform distribution
Mean difference (d̄)
Large Counts condition (two samples)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Uniform distribution".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The long-run average value of a random variable, calculated as the weighted mean of all possible values: E(X) = Σ[x·P(x)]"?

options.text: Expected value E(X)
Within-block variability
Chi-square statistic
Independent events

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Expected value E(X)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The square root of variance, measuring the typical deviation of a random variable from its expected value: σ = √Var(X)"?

options.text: CLT for proportions
Residual plot
Standard deviation of a random variable
Joint distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Standard deviation of a random variable".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F(x) = P(X ≤ x); gives the probability that a random variable takes a value less than or equal to x"?

options.text: t-distribution
Standard normal distribution
Cumulative distribution function (CDF)
Power

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Cumulative distribution function (CDF)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Distributions is accurate?

options.text: The width of the interval is determined by the confidence level (higher → wider), sample size (larger → narrower), and sample variability (larger s → wider).
Distributions describe the pattern of values a variable takes and how frequently each value occurs.

The three major types—selection bias, response bias, and nonresponse bias—must be addressed through careful study design, while confounding variables create false associations that mask or mimic true causal relationships.

Correlation and Causation is a critical distinction in statistics: correlation measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship, but it does not imply that one variable causes changes in the other.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Distributions?

options.text: One-sided tests (Hₐ: p > p₀ or p < p₀) and two-sided tests (Hₐ: p ≠ p₀) require different p-value calculations.
Residuals and Least-Squares Regression fit a linear model ŷ = a + bx to bivariate data by minimizing the sum of squared residuals (Σ(y - ŷ)²).

Key distributions include the uniform distribution (all outcomes equally likely), binomial distribution (counts of successes in fixed trials), and normal distribution (bell-shaped continuous outcomes).
The slope b = r(sy/sx) represents the predicted change in y for a one-unit increase in x, while the y-intercept a gives the predicted y when x = 0.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Distributions?

options.text: The test statistic t = d̄/(sd/√n) with df = n-1 (where n is the number of pairs) tests H₀: μd = 0.
Choosing the correct probability distribution model for a given situation is fundamental to making valid statistical inferences.
Understanding this distinction is critical—many important questions (smoking's effect on health, educational interventions) rely on observational data when experiments are unethical or impractical.

Correlation and Causation is a critical distinction in statistics: correlation measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship, but it does not imply that one variable causes changes in the other.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Distributions?

options.text: Distributions describe the pattern of values a variable takes and how frequently each value occurs.
The CLT also applies to sample proportions and sums.
A randomized block design combines the benefits of both techniques: blocking controls for known confounders while randomization balances unknown confounders across treatment groups.
Range, IQR, and standard deviation each capture different aspects of spread, and choosing the right measure depends on the data's shape and the presence of outliers.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Distributions?

options.text: Controlling includes keeping conditions identical across groups, standardizing procedures, and using control groups as baselines for comparison.
Conditional probability is fundamental to updating beliefs with new information and is the basis of Bayes' Theorem, which reverses conditional probabilities.

Key distributions include the uniform distribution (all outcomes equally likely), binomial distribution (counts of successes in fixed trials), and normal distribution (bell-shaped continuous outcomes).

Probability quantifies uncertainty on a scale from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain), with the Law of Large Numbers stating that relative frequency approaches true probability as the number of trials increases.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Distributions?

options.text: News organizations reporting poll results with margin of error ('52% favor the measure ± 3 percentage points') derived from the confidence interval formula
Combining manufacturing tolerances: if a bolt and nut are produced independently, the play in the assembled joint has variance equal to the sum of their individual variances
Quality inspection using the binomial distribution to calculate the probability of finding defective items in a batch sample
Spam email filtering using conditional probability to classify messages based on the frequency of certain words in spam vs. legitimate emails (Bayesian classification)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Distributions?

options.text: Modeling customer arrival times at a store using appropriate probability distributions to optimize staffing schedules
Epidemiological prospective cohort studies (like the Framingham Heart Study) tracking thousands of people over decades to identify cardiovascular risk factors
Evaluating whether a new teaching method causes improved test scores requires an experiment with random assignment—simply comparing schools using different methods is observational
College admissions comparing applicants who took different standardized tests (SAT vs. ACT) by converting to common z-score scale

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Distributions applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Quality control in manufacturing using μ ± 3σ control limits to detect when a production process drifts out of specification
Medical trials comparing average recovery times between a new surgical technique and the standard procedure across two independent patient groups
Financial risk modeling using probability distributions to estimate potential portfolio losses (Value at Risk calculations)
Analyzing biological measurements (human heights, birth weights) that naturally approximate normal distributions to identify atypical values

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Distributions?

options.text: The classic ice cream and drowning correlation: both increase in summer due to the lurking variable of temperature, not because ice cream causes drowning
Quality inspection using the binomial distribution to calculate the probability of finding defective items in a batch sample
College admissions comparing applicants who took different standardized tests (SAT vs. ACT) by converting to common z-score scale
Evaluating whether a new teaching method causes improved test scores requires an experiment with random assignment—simply comparing schools using different methods is observational

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Distributions in practice?

options.text: Medical screening: computing the probability of getting at least one false positive in multiple independent tests using the complement rule: P(at least one) = 1 - P(none)
Modeling customer arrival times at a store using appropriate probability distributions to optimize staffing schedules
Economists checking LINE conditions before publishing a regression model of how education level predicts income
Clinical trials comparing cure rates between drug and placebo groups to determine whether the drug is more effective than no treatment

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Distributions.
